descriptions: if a thought or idea occurs to you, you suddenly and unexpectedly start to think about it
examples: It didn’t occur to her to ask how he’d found her.
It suddenly occurred to her that Joe was afraid of being alone.
The thought of giving up never occurred to me.
highlighted_examples: It didn’t [occur] [to] her to ask how he’d found her.
It suddenly [occurred] [to] her that Joe was afraid of being alone.
The thought of giving up never [occurred] [to] me.

descriptions: to give thanks, praise, or prayers to God
to provide something that is intended to impress, please, or satisfy someone
examples: Lord, we offer up our prayers to you.
The friendly staff offer up a real taste of Southwest America.
highlighted_examples: Lord, we [offer] [up] our prayers to you.
The friendly staff [offer] [up] a real taste of Southwest America.

descriptions: if a discussion opens out, or if you open it out, it becomes more general or it starts to include more subjects
if a path or passage opens out, it becomes wider at the end
if something opens out, or if you open it out, it opens from a folded position so that you can see its shape
examples: He tried to open out the conversation.
She opened the map out on the table.
The alleyway opened out into a courtyard behind the houses.
The buds on the trees were starting to open out.
They were too open out here, no protection from the dangerous, lightening sky.
highlighted_examples: He tried to [open] [out] the conversation.
She [opened] the map [out] on the table.
The alleyway [opened] [out] into a courtyard behind the houses.
The buds on the trees were starting to [open] [out].
They were too [open] [out] here, no protection from the dangerous, lightening sky.

descriptions: if a discussion opens up, or if you open it up, it becomes more general or starts to include more subjects
if something opens up, or if you open it up, it opens from a folded position so that you can see its shape
same as open
to create a difference between the people or groups involved in something
to create a new opportunity or possibility
to cut into someone’s body for medical reasons
to make it easier to travel or do business in a country
to open a locked door, container, or building
to start shooting a gun
to talk more about your personal feelings and experiences
examples: Breathing hard, listening to Titus open up the bar, curled around my lonely pillow, I remember her saying those fatal words.
China is opening up to foreign investment.
Donald wants to retire and open up a bookshop.
Going freelance opens up all sorts of possibilities to you.
He opened up on the crowd with an automatic rifle.
He opens up the shop every morning.
I think we need to open up the discussion and ask members of the audience for their opinions.
It’s taken a few months, but Katy is finally starting to open up to me.
New cinemas are starting to open up.
New markets are opening up every day.
Open up! This is the police!
She concentrated her attention on getting Richard to open up about himself.
She opened her umbrella up at the first drops of rain.
The building of canals opened up the interior of the country.
The issue of Europe has opened up deep divisions within the party.
The rose buds are beginning to open up.
United have opened up a 6-point lead over their nearest rivals.
What he said seemed to open up vistas, disquieting views into a landscape she did not like.
When they opened her up they found a huge tumour.
Which is why I asked him to try to get you to open up to me.
highlighted_examples: Breathing hard, listening to Titus [open] [up] the bar, curled around my lonely pillow, I remember her saying those fatal words.
China is [opening] [up] to foreign investment.
Donald wants to retire and [open] [up] a bookshop.
Going freelance [opens] [up] all sorts of possibilities to you.
He [opened] [up] on the crowd with an automatic rifle.
He [opens] [up] the shop every morning.
I think we need to [open] [up] the discussion and ask members of the audience for their opinions.
It’s taken a few months, but Katy is finally starting to [open] [up] to me.
New cinemas are starting to [open] [up].
New markets are [opening] [up] every day.
[Open] [up]! This is the police!
She concentrated her attention on getting Richard to [open] [up] about himself.
She [opened] her umbrella [up] at the first drops of rain.
The building of canals [opened] [up] the interior of the country.
The issue of Europe has [opened] [up] deep divisions within the party.
The rose buds are beginning to [open] [up].
United have [opened] [up] a 6-point lead over their nearest rivals.
What he said seemed to [open] [up] vistas, disquieting views into a landscape she did not like.
When they [opened] her [up] they found a huge tumour.
Which is why I asked him to try to get you to [open] [up] to me.

descriptions: to make a choice or decision from a range of possibilities
examples: After recent setbacks in the market, most people are now opting for low-risk investments.
married couples opting to be taxed as two single persons
highlighted_examples: After recent setbacks in the market, most people are now [opting] [for] low-risk investments.
married couples [opting] to be taxed as two single persons

descriptions: to decide that you want to do something or be involved in something
examples: Members are given the choice to opt in.
The British government was reluctant to opt into such a system.
highlighted_examples: Members are given the choice to [opt] [in].
The British government was reluctant to [opt] into such a system.

descriptions: to decide that you want to do something or be involved in something
examples: Members are given the choice to opt in.
The British government was reluctant to opt into such a system.
highlighted_examples: Members are given the choice to [opt] in.
The British government was reluctant to [opt] [into] such a system.

descriptions: to decide not to take part in something or to stop taking part in it
examples: I had asked Father Whittaker if I might opt out of taking part in the service to act as chief mourner since the old lady had no relatives.
If you opt out before December 1st, your deposit will be refunded.
The firm opted out of the company car scheme last year.
highlighted_examples: I had asked Father Whittaker if I might [opt] [out] of taking part in the service to act as chief mourner since the old lady had no relatives.
If you [opt] [out] before December 1st, your deposit will be refunded.
The firm [opted] [out] of the company car scheme last year.

descriptions: to order food from a restaurant and ask for it to be delivered to you
examples: He led the horse to the pike, tethered it, then arranged the planks he'd collected in order of length.
I ordered in pizza for dinner.
It needed a credible threat in order to justify its massive expenditure.
Only the Professor sought out his company, and then only in order to play chess.
We were too tired to cook, so we ordered in.
highlighted_examples: He led the horse to the pike, tethered it, then arranged the planks he'd collected in [order] of length.
I [ordered] [in] pizza for dinner.
It needed a credible threat in [order] to justify its massive expenditure.
Only the Professor sought out his company, and then only in [order] to play chess.
We were too tired to cook, so we [ordered] [in].

descriptions: to tell a player officially to leave the sports field because they have done something that is not allowed by the rules
examples: The referee had no option but to order him off.
highlighted_examples: The referee had no option but to [order] him [off].

descriptions: to order food from a restaurant and ask for it to be delivered to you
examples: Familiar by now with English ways, he reckoned he knew which gestures were out of order.
Or perhaps the bell was out of order, making it necessary to leave the gate unlocked.
We got back late and decided to order out.
We're looking for the place where its machinery is out of order, for the key item.
highlighted_examples: Familiar by now with English ways, he reckoned he knew which gestures were out of [order].
Or perhaps the bell was out of [order], making it necessary to leave the gate unlocked.

descriptions: in a hotel, to choose food or drinks and ask for them to be delivered to your room
to tell someone to do something, provide something, or deliver something
examples: I decided to order up breakfast.
The Pentagon ordered up cruise missile attacks.
We ordered up FBI reports on the men.
highlighted_examples: I decided to [order] [up] breakfast.
The Pentagon [ordered] [up] cruise missile attacks.
We [ordered] [up] FBI reports on the men.

descriptions: to admit that you have done something bad or embarrassing
examples: Dan could have made this whole thing go away simply by owning up.
Two local students later owned up to the prank.
highlighted_examples: Dan could have made this whole thing go away simply by [owning] [up].
Two local students later [owned] [up] to the prank.
